data.title: MLPI: Collect A Double-Digit Yield From This MLP ETF, Without The K-1 Headache
data.description:
Overview

I've held MLPs (Master Limited Partnerships) within my dividend portfolio because of their unique tax benefits and sector exposure. MLPs have the ability to offer higher than average dividend yield because they are legally required to generate at least 90% of their income from qualifying sources. This makes this a great tool within an income focused portfolio and a good fit for investors that want to limit their overall tax liability. However, I've limited my exposure to different MLPs in the last year because dealing with multiple Schedule K-1 forms during tax time can become a headache.

Neos MLP & Energy Infrastructure High Income ETF (MLPI) now offers investors a way to get direct exposure to this unique asset class, while providing diversity and eliminating K-1 form. MLPI is still relatively new with an inception dating back to December of 2025. Looking at the performance since the fund's inception, we can see that MLPI's share price has increased by about 3.2%. When including the distributions that were paid out to shareholders so far, the total return jumps up to nearly 6% over the same time frame. MLPI now offers an estimated annual dividend yield above 15% and issues payouts on a monthly basis.

NEOS has maintained an excellent track record within the high-yield option ETF space, so I am excited to see how this fund performs over a longer holding period. MLPI is able to provide this high yield due to the inclusion of an option writing strategy that's deployed against its assets. While this allows an amplified income to be collected, there are also some tradeoffs that investors should consider before initiating a position. For instance, MLPI may not be the best choice for investors looking to maximize total returns from the sector. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that MLPI implements.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, MLPI has total net assets of $110.4M that are spread across a diverse range of master limited partnerships. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 0.68%, which is fairly reasonable considering the high dividend yield being offered to investors. MLPI seeks to distribute a high monthly income from MLPs and other energy infrastructure companies. The fund also combines strategic tax loss harvesting opportunities, as well as receiving pass-through tax benefits from the MLP structure itself.

What I like about MLPI's strategy is that it specifically implements an OTM (out-of-the-money) strategy, which allows for greater upside capital appreciation. This is because an OTM strategy means that the selected strike price is typically above the current market price of the asset that the option is based on. Rather than write options against its own holdings, the fund's prospectus specifically states that it writes options against 'MLP ETFs' but it doesn't specifically list out any names. I would assume that this leaves the fund pretty flexible in terms of which ETF it can write options on, such as something like Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) or even the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE).

When looking at the top holdings of the fund, we can see that MLPI provides direct equity exposure to some of the most notable names in the sector. For instance, The Williams Companies (WMB) is the largest holding at 9.76% of assets. This is followed by exposure to many other high quality names throughout the sector, including Enbridge (ENB), TC Energy (TRP), Kinder Morgan (KMI), ONEOK (OKE), and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), just to name a few.

Although MLPI provides direct exposure to these companies, investors should limit their expectations for MLPI to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation. Despite an OTM strategy being implemented, the upside growth is still capped at whatever the selected strike price is. So if MLPI writes an option on an ETF priced at $50 per share, it may select a strike price at $55 per share. While this allows for some upside growth, MLPI will not capture any growth above the strike price.

Performance Insights & Risk

Since the fund's history is so short, we have a limited operating window to assess its performance so far. When running MLPI's performance against some ETFs like AMLP and XLE, we can see that MLPI has the lowest level of price growth over the month. So although the energy sector has run higher, this is already an indication that MLPI's structure will cap the upside growth over a prolonged holding period. In comparison, both AMLP and XLE are structured as traditional ETFs so they are able to capture the complete upside of their holdings.

When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, MLPI slightly outperforms AMLP since its inception. While MLPI's high double-digit distribution may lead to outperformance during specific market conditions, I believe that the outlook can be summarized by three scenarios. This is why MLPI may not be the best buy-and-hold investment for long-term investors that want to maximize their total return. The fund's strategy means that MLPI is likely to perform as such:

  • MLPs Rise In Value = MLPI will also rise in value since the fund has direct equity exposure to its holdings. The option writing strategy will likely produce large option premiums and distributions are most likely to stay within a consistent range. However, MLPI will underperform traditional MLP ETFs due to the nature of the capped upside.
  • MLPs Trade Flat = This is where MLPI has the best chance of outperforming traditional ETFs like AMLP. MLPI's share price may stay within a consistent range and if the option writing strategy is successful, the large premiums can result in outperformance.
  • MLPs Decline In Value = MLPI will also decline. Although the options cap upside growth, it does not protect against downside movements. While its entirely possible that distributions can soften the rate of declines, MLPI can still suffer from NAV erosion if option premiums aren't generating enough income to support distributions. Therefore, distributions can also fall if MLPs experience a prolonged decline.

Therefore, the best scenario would be for MLPs to trade mostly flat or slightly increase over time. If MLPs rise too fast, MLPI will severely underperform over a longer holding period. Additionally, the fund will struggle to recover from any sharp drops in the MLP market, since the option writing strategy caps upside growth. So to manage these risks, I believe that MLPI is most ideal when used as an accompanying position for an investor that already has exposure to MLPs, whether that's through an ETF like AMLP or individual holdings.

By implementing the fund with this approach, investors that want to grow their capital can better participate during bull markets. Additionally, MLPI's diversity can also act as a hedge during unfavorable market cycles since it eliminates single stock risk. Plus, MLPs tend to demonstrate better resistance against cyclical shifts in the market compared to traditional growth stocks.

Tax-Efficient Dividend Potential

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.6608 per share, the estimated forward annual dividend yield sits around 15.4%. Since the fund has only issued two distributions since its inception, we can only calculate the dividend yield on a forward basis. Unfortunately, we have no history to boldly claim that MLPI will offer excellent dividend consistency over time. However, I anticipate that NEOS will follow the same priority of consistent payouts as it does with its other covered call ETFs. I plan to keep track of the payouts over the next year and will revisit the fund after we have an established payout history.

The primary risk for the MLPI is a scenario where the sector experiences a downturn. If the fund finds itself in a position where it is frequently paying out more than it earns, the distributions will have to be adjusted downward as the underlying NAV of the fund declines. This is just the nature of these covered call ETFs that rely on option premiums to fund distributions, so this is a risk that investors should be aware of.

On a more positive note, one of the main benefits of MLPs is their unique tax structure that allows investors to defer taxes of all dividends received. However, this usually involves investors to receive a Schedule K-1 Form that must be reported during tax time. Having filled these out for several years, they can be a headache but the most frustrating part is that these forms mailed out in mid-March. So investors need to delay the filing of their taxes and this leaves little room for planning.

MLPI captures a similar benefit but by strategically using return of capital distributions. Unlike traditional distributions supported by net investment income, return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. Referring to the latest section 19(a) notice, 100% of the distributions paid so far have been classified as return of capital.

This makes MLPI an excellent choice for investors that want exposure to MLPs and their tax-benefits, but don't want to deal with Schedule K-1 Forms. This also enables investors some flexibility in terms of which investment account they can utilize MLPI in. Investors that decide to hold MLPI within a regular taxable brokerage account may be able to effectively defer taxes for a long period of time. In theory, it's possible to hold MLPI for long enough until an investor's cost basis reaches $0 per share. At this point, all distributions received would be classified as long-term capital gains, which are still tax-efficient.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that MLPI is a compelling alternative for investors that want exposure to MLPs without the hassle of the added paperwork at tax time. The fund provides direct equity exposure to a diverse range of MLPs, while implementing a covered call strategy against MLP ETFs. While this can cap the fund's upside growth potential, it also allows MLPI to generate an amplified level of income during times where the sector is trading flat. Additionally, MLPI's focus on return of capital distributions means that the distributions received are still considered to be tax-efficient over time. Therefore, I believe that MLPI can be a solid long-term holding for investors looking to minimize their tax liability.

data.title: U.S. Fixed Income: Policy-Driven Opportunities Emerge
data.description:
By Ashok Bhatia, CFA and Jason W. Smith

Amid the administration’s flurry of affordability-targeted policy initiatives, bond investment opportunities are emerging.

Episodes like last week’s geopolitical flare-up over Greenland crank up the global macro noise and volatility that have been with us for some time, making it easy to be distracted by headlines. Yet as similar bouts of short-term market turbulence emerge through the year, as we expect, the real challenge is to stay anchored in fundamentals, focused on long-term outcomes and attentive to smaller policy developments at home that can move markets in more durable—and investable—ways.

In U.S. fixed income, we are seeing this play out in certain asset classes—most notably agency mortgage‑backed securities (MBS), where the opportunity, in our view, is worth focusing on.

Several forces are at work: the Federal Reserve’s easing bias, still-elevated (if tighter) spreads and improving technicals. The most powerful, however, is the administration’s recently announced $200 billion MBS purchase program to be executed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, the government-sponsored enterprises (GSEs) at the center of the U.S. mortgage market.

Announced on January 8, the program is essentially designed to help support U.S. households and stimulate the housing market by creating greater demand for agency MBS, thereby compressing mortgage spreads and pushing mortgage rates lower.

In isolation, this may look like a sizable initiative, but against the backdrop of the much bigger macro shifts underway in the U.S. and globally, it could easily be overlooked—precisely why we think it deserves closer attention.

Why We See an Opportunity Now

The reason is simple: A large, policy-driven buyer is returning to a market where many investors continue to seek high-quality fixed income.

Even before the formal announcement, markets had begun to anticipate that the GSEs would become more active buyers. In the fourth quarter of 2025, agency MBS spreads started to tighten as investors increasingly priced in additional Fannie and Freddie demand. The January announcement has now turned that expectation into an explicit commitment.

For the past couple of years, agency MBS have mainly been bought by spread-sensitive managers rotating in because mortgages looked cheap versus corporate bonds and other credit. Now, with the administration signaling that Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac will use their balance sheets more aggressively, the marginal buyer is shifting from opportunistic private capital to steady public sector demand. When that buyer’s primary mandate is to own these bonds rather than to trade relative value, spreads tend not just to tighten but to remain contained, if not grind tighter.

Scale also matters. The $200 billion headline broadly matches the remaining capacity the GSEs have under current regulatory caps. If more were deemed necessary, raising those caps is at least a plausible next step. That possibility is one reason the market views more consistent support as the risk rather than less consistent support.

All this is happening while spreads, though off their wides, still sit above longer-term averages, and interest rate volatility has declined as the Fed’s path has become clearer. In our view, that mix of supportive policy, still-attractive risk premia and a calmer rates backdrop creates a sensible entry point to add high-quality fixed income before the value becomes more widely recognized.

Balancing Quality and Income

We do not expect this program to transform U.S. housing or send mortgage rates sharply lower on its own. Treasury yields will remain the main driver of what borrowers pay.

Instead, we expect something more measured and investable: spreads moving from “cheap” toward “fair”, and possibly a little richer, as the program is implemented and potentially extended. That gradual tightening can be uncomfortable for investors waiting for a pullback, but it can be rewarding for those prepared to own the sector and let carry work over time.

Importantly, agency MBS also continue to offer a compelling balance of quality and income. In a world where credit risk has been quiet but is starting to build, they remain a high-quality, government-backed option for investors who want fixed income without corporate credit exposure. The risk premium is not as generous as it was at its widest, but we believe it remains attractive given the underlying support.

One word of caution, low interest rate volatility has been a tailwind for the sector. If volatility were to rise again—whether because of the Fed, the long end of the Treasury market or external shocks—that would matter for mortgages. Being clear about where we are in the rate volatility cycle is therefore a key part of our risk assessment.

Leaning Into the Backstop

With credit risk likely to become more of a focus over time, we think investors should pay attention when a high-quality, government-backed asset class gains a fresh source of official support. Agency MBS are no longer outright cheap and the Fed’s rate-cutting cycle is more advanced, but that is exactly why this phase is about locking in resilient income where fundamentals, policy and market technicals are aligned.

We believe U.S. agency MBS now sits firmly in that category. Our approach is to be prudent but active: selective on structure and coupon, alert to prepayment and interest rate volatility risks, yet willing to build exposure into a market where public sector demand is likely to do much of the heavy lifting.

What to Watch For

  • Monday 1/26:
    • U.S. Durable Goods Order
    • U.S. Atlanta Fed GDPNow
  • Tuesday 1/27:
    • U.S. CB Consumer Confidence
  • Wednesday 1/28:
    • Canada BoC Interest Rate Decision
    • U.S. Federal Reserve Interest Rate Decision
  • Thursday 1/29:
    • U.S. Initial Jobless Claims
  • Friday 1/30:
    • Eurozone GDP
    • Eurozone Unemployment Rate
    • U.S. Producer Price Index

data.title: CMS Energy: Let This Utility Light Up Your Portfolio With Gains
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

In his 1949 classic The Intelligent Investor, the father of value investing, Benjamin Graham, laid out a framework that prioritized financial stability and long-term reliability. One of the most memorable quantitative benchmarks was the requirement for a company to have a record of uninterrupted dividend payments for at least 20 consecutive years.

Of course, that's not always possible for companies with shorter corporate histories. However, it's something that I view as a fantastic starting point for more established companies with an added twist. I like to cover companies with either 20-plus-year dividend growth streaks or companies that I can envision making it to such a feat. In my view, this is the gold standard for separating the best compounders from the rest.

That brings me to my focus for today, which is CMS Energy (CMS). When Kody last covered the utility for Brad Thomas with a "Buy" rating in January 2025, he thought that economic development in Michigan was a positive for the regulated utility's five-year capital spending plan. At the time, he thought shares were discounted as well.

Almost a year later, I'm reiterating Kody's "Buy" rating. CMS's current five-year spending plan is $20 billion, which is up from $17 billion for 2024 through 2028. This is also likely to be raised further on the upcoming Q4 2025 earnings call, which should support high single-digit annual adjusted EPS growth. CMS possesses a BBB+ S&P credit rating with a stable outlook, too. The final support for my "Buy" case is the fact that shares are priced at a double-digit percentage discount to my fair value estimate.

CMS Energy Has A Bright Future Ahead

On Oct. 30, CMS released its financial results for the third quarter ended Sept. 30, 2025. The company's operating revenue jumped 15.9% year-over-year to $2.02 billion during the quarter. For more perspective, that was $210 million greater than Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus in the quarter.

In absolute dollar terms, a 16% uptick in electric utility operating revenue to $1.68 billion was the driving force for the third quarter. According to CFO Rejji Hayes' opening remarks during the Q3 2025 Earnings Call, this was largely made possible by a constructive outcome achieved in an electric rate order received in March.

A 31.7% spike in NorthStar Clean Energy's operating revenue to $108 million led the way in percentage terms during the third quarter. That was fueled by a mix of higher revenue from leasing and operating revenue from independent power production and energy commodity sales.

Finally, gas utility operating revenue grew by 9.9% to $234 million in the third quarter. That was fueled by a combination of gas rate increases and a higher cost of gas sold (which is passed onto consumers).

Moving to the bottom line, CMS logged $0.93 in adjusted EPS for the third quarter. This was up 10.7% over the year-ago period and came in $0.07 ahead of Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus during the quarter. A slight increase in CMS's diluted share count and a 60 basis point contraction in the non-GAAP net profit margin to 13.8% is why adjusted EPS growth lagged operating revenue growth in the quarter.

Looking out over the next few years, CMS has a path to sustained adjusted EPS growth.

The company plans to spend $20 billion on expanding and upgrading its infrastructure from 2025 through 2029. This includes $8.5 billion for electric distribution spending, $6.3 billion for gas utility spending, and a remaining $5.2 billion for clean energy generation. That's likely to take CMS's rate base from $26.2 billion in 2024 to $39.4 billion by 2029 - an 8.5% CAGR. This is what supports the company's long-term annual adjusted EPS growth target of between 6% and 8%.

Better yet, CMS's capex is likely to accelerate even further. That could potentially provide further support to the high single-digit annual adjusted EPS growth Kody was anticipating.

President and CEO Garrick Rochow noted months ago on the Q2 2025 Earnings Call that CMS has an agreement with a data center with up to 1 gigawatt of load planning. This will come to its service territory beginning in early 2030 and ramp up from there.

On the Q3 earnings call, he also indicated that there are other large data centers in the final and advanced stages of development. This is why CMS's five-year capex spending plan is likely to be revised several billion dollars higher again for 2026 through 2030 on the upcoming Q4 earnings call in several weeks.

That also supports the current FAST Graphs consensus. The FAST Graphs consensus for 2025 is for adjusted EPS to grow by 7.5% to $3.59. In 2026, another 7.2% increase to $3.85 is being projected. For 2027, an additional 7.8% jump in adjusted EPS to $4.15 is anticipated. In 2028, adjusted EPS is expected to climb by 7.5% to $4.46.

CMS is financially stable as well. The company's interest coverage ratio through the first nine months of 2025 was 2.6x. For a regulated utility, this is decent. That's how it earns a BBB+ issuer credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook. This provides the company with the low cost of capital necessary to accretively fund its capex in the coming years (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to CMS's Q3 2025 Earnings Press Release, CMS's Q3 2025 10-Q Filing, or CMS's November 2025 Investor Presentation).

Moderate Upside To Fair Value

In the last 12 months, shares of CMS have logged a 9% total return. This has trailed the 18% total return posted by the S&P 500 index (SP500) over that time.

Even following its rally, the regulated utility is trading at a current-year P/E ratio of 18.1. That's modestly cheaper than the previous value of 18.3 and meaningfully below the 10-year average P/E ratio of 21.3, according to data from FAST Graphs.

Moving forward, I'm upping my fair value multiple slightly from Kody's 19.3 (one standard deviation below the 10-year average P/E ratio) to around 20. What is my rationale for doing so?

Well, the FAST Graphs annual forward-adjusted EPS growth consensus remains unchanged at 7.5%. Such a growth rate would already be greater than the 10-year average CAGR of 6.6%. It gets better, though: I believe there's even more visibility into how CMS generates such an earnings growth rate. This is because the company is likely to substantially up its five-year capital spending plan on its upcoming Q4 earnings call.

The elevated interest rate environment remains a drag on CMS, fully reverting to its 10-year average P/E ratio. The 10-year U.S. Treasury (US10Y) is currently yielding almost 4.2%. This is above the 10-year average yield of around 2.5%, per Macrotrends. Looking ahead, I believe the 10-year yield will remain in the high-3% to low-4% range.

Currently, the calendar year 2026 is 4% in the books. That implies another 96% of 2026 and 4% of 2027 still lie ahead over the coming 12 months. This yields a forward 12-month adjusted EPS input of $3.86.

Applying my fair value P/E ratio of 20, I arrive at a fair value of $77 a share. Relative to the current $70 share price (as of January 13th, 2026), that's equivalent to a 10% discount to fair value.

If CMS lives up to growth forecasts and reverts to fair value, it could put up a 14% total return in 2026. By the end of 2030, the regulated utility could produce roughly 11% annual total returns.

CMS Energy Has More Dividend Growth Ahead

CMS sports a 3.1% forward dividend yield, which registers about 30 basis points below the 3.4% median forward yield of the utility sector. This represents a 40 basis point narrowing over the past 12 months. That's why Seeking Alpha's Quant System awards C grades for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield, respectively (up from C- and D+ grades in January 2025).

Putting aside its middle-of-the-road starting income, CMS stands out everywhere else. Perhaps most notably, the company has boosted its payout for 18 consecutive years. This is better than the sector median of 11 years and puts it on track to become a Dividend Aristocrat in 2032. That accounts for the A- grade from the Quant System for overall dividend consistency (unchanged from January 2025).

Why am I so confident that CMS is a Dividend Aristocrat in the making? Well, the company's adjusted EPS payout ratio is set to be in the low-60% range for 2026. According to the Zen Research Terminal, this would be comfortably better than the 75% payout ratio that rating agencies desire from the industry.

Thus, the Zen Research Terminal pegs the probability of a dividend cut at just 0.7% over the next 12 months. In the next average recession, the risk remains subdued at 2.5%. That's why the Quant System issues an A- grade for overall dividend safety (up from a B+ grade in January 2025).

In the coming years, the Quant System anticipates a 5.6% annual forward dividend per share growth rate. This would be roughly 30 basis points above the sector median of 5.3%. I would even argue that this projection is slightly conservative. That's because CMS is committing to keeping the payout ratio right around 60% (where it is right now). This implies dividend growth could be 50 or even 100 basis points above such a level. Regardless, CMS earns a B- grade from the Quant System for overall dividend growth (down from a B+ grade in January 2025).

Risks To Consider

The fundamental outlook for CMS is encouraging. However, there are risks to the investment thesis.

As Kody has outlined in prior articles, the most notable risk to the regulated utility arguably stems from its operations being entirely in Michigan. On the one hand, CMS has managed to thrive under both Republican and Democratic administrations. The risk is that this may not continue indefinitely.

That's especially the case when considering a recent AARP survey of Americans age 50 and older. In this survey, at least 3 out of 4 respondents were worried about the cost of utilities going up. An overwhelming majority (83%) were concerned about higher electricity rates.

This could be an issue because the 50-and-up voting bloc holds significant influence. That could force the hand of Michigan's regulators to appear like they're playing "hardball" with regulated utilities. This could lead to credit rating downgrades from rating agencies, which would raise the cost of capital for CMS. That could put its current capital spending plan in jeopardy, which could undermine the utility's growth potential.

Any potent natural disasters concentrated in Michigan could also disrupt operations for CMS. This could temporarily weigh on its financial results.

Finally, CMS is the largest regulated utility in Michigan. That makes it an attractive target for would-be hackers. If any attempted cyber breaches are widely successful, CMS could be hit with litigation by affected parties and penalized by regulators.

Conclusion

CMS is a top-notch utility. This is evidenced by its solid business growth over the last two decades in environments led by both Republican and Democratic administrations. Given economic development in Michigan and the regulated utility's planned capex, I expect the coming years to be no different in this aspect. CMS continues to possess an investment-grade balance sheet as well.

Lastly, shares are trading at a moderate discount to my fair value estimate. This sets up CMS stock for a potential low-teens total return in 2026 and 10%+ annual total returns through 2030. That's why I'm sticking with Kody's "Buy" rating.

Build a fortress-strength portfolio that will provide outsized returns and strong dividend growth. Let CMS light up your fortress, and live like a Dividend King.

data.title: Alcoa's Post-Q4 Earnings Dip Offers A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Alcoa's Post-Earnings Dip Shouldn't Have Happened

It's been about 9 months since I last looked at Alcoa Corporation (AA) stock. In my previous update, I confirmed my earlier “Buy” rating despite the stock's continued correction, and it looks like my call has already aged extremely well:

The company reported its Q4 results on Friday, beating the consensus estimates quite strongly (especially on the top line versus previous quarters' beats).

Some analysts were quick to react to their earnings, calling the beat “less impressive than it seems” (I'm citing UBS here).

The stock was over 9% down throughout the trading session but eventually closed only 1.47% lower.

While I agree that there's little to be happy about regarding headline figures alone and that UBS analysts are right about some adjustments that led to the bottom-line beat in Q4, Alcoa seems to be finally coming out of its heavy restructuring period and is now moving into one of aggressive cash generation and portfolio optimization. I think the market has misunderstood the Q4 updates and focused too much on the EPS calculations that took place after the print, missing the forest for the trees. The stock remains cheap, and the catalysts/events that I anticipate throughout the next couple of years should lead to meaningful FCF generation and value creation for shareholders. The recovery cycle for Alcoa may be just getting started.

What Makes Me Bullish On Alcoa?

Of course, in addition to the sales and EPS beats, I like that Alcoa's FCF generation is improving amid the management's constant focus on deleveraging and returns to shareholders.

But the above data is what has already happened. Ahead, there are a few reasons for staying bullish on AA that I see, and not all of them are directly tied to just plain financials like FCF, margins, or net debt to EBITDA ratios.

First, this year, Alcoa is entering the long-anticipated normalization cycle for some of its most troubled assets. For example, in 2024 and 2025, Alcoa's San Ciprian complex in Spain was dragging down consolidated earnings, and even for full-year 2026, management guided for an EBITDA loss of $75-100 million. However, by mid-2026, the situation should flip, and Alcoa wants to complete the smelter restart, which might reach a full annual capacity of 228 kmt once done. Following this logic, we can expect that by the end of 2026, we should see profitability on both EBITDA and net income from that project, while by 2027, the complex is on a path toward cash neutrality.

For the smelter, we will reach profitability after we complete the restart, and that is still on track for the middle of 2026. The pricing is very favorable there. We’re still working on our overall program for the complex, and I can give you an update on our EBITDA guidance for 2026 for the combined smelter and refinery. So we have an EBITDA loss of approximately $75-$100 million. The majority of that is the refinery. Our free cash flow consumption will be approximately $100-$130 million, and that includes refinery CapEx of about $50 million. We are making some working capital improvements across the site, so we have the benefit of that as well. We are still progressing on our plan to reach cash neutrality in 2027.

Source: AA's earnings call

Second, what's been driving down the narrative and AA's multiples, in addition to some earnings erosion in the past, was the regulatory approval of its rolling five-year mine plan in Western Australia. Now, as we're heading into 2026, the company should get rid of this regulatory risk, as the WA EPA (“Western Australia Environmental Protection Authority”) is set to publish its assessment by June 2026, “with final Ministerial decisions expected by year-end 2026.” Having achieved this approval, Alcoa won't immediately change its bauxite grades, but the very fact should provide more regulatory certainty and hence help the valuation multiple to expand after the risk relief.

Third, since the beginning of January 2026, the EU has implemented CBAM (“Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism”), which is designed to place duties on aluminum imports based on their carbon footprint, with the main target being US producers.

Alcoa has an advantage here because ~86% of its smelters are already powered by renewable energy, so with competitors now being obligated to pay quite high carbon taxes, the management expects a net positive impact of ~$10 per metric ton in FY2026, as the expected Rotterdam premium uplift ($40/t) will outweigh Alcoa's minor carbon cost increases.

In Europe, the Rotterdam regional premium increased in the fourth quarter, partially due to demand front loading ahead of CBAM's implementation in January 2026. CBAM implementation is expected to deliver a net benefit to Alcoa in 2026 because the anticipated increase in the Rotterdam premium should more than offset our incremental carbon emissions costs.

Under CBAM, foreign importers to Europe must purchase CBAM certificates to compensate for their carbon emissions. This increase in cost reflects the need for Europe in aluminum-deficit region to price imports high enough to attract marginal foreign producers that must now purchase CBAM certificates. Industry analysts estimate that CBAM could add roughly $40 per metric ton to the Rotterdam premium in 2026, and we believe some of this uplift was already included into the fourth quarter of 2025.

Source: AA's earnings call

Fourth, the macro setup is getting more favorable, specifically for aluminum suppliers, after global primary aluminum inventories have fallen to their lowest year-end levels in at least 15 years. The regions where Alcoa is mainly represented should feel the main scarcity effect:

From the management's commentary, we know that every $100/mt increase in aluminum prices adds $215 million to Alcoa's EBITDA, so if the above setups lead to a continued rally in this commodity, Alcoa's going to be quite well-leveraged to capitalize on that in a timely fashion.

Continuing to speak about Alcoa's EBITDA, but in the stock's valuation context, it's hard to ignore the fact that AA trades at just ~6.4x forward EV/EBITDA ratio, which is about 40% below the median Materials company.

And that low EV/EBITDA takes place amid an improving EBITDA growth outlook. In FY2027, for example, AA's EBITDA is projected to reach $3.464 billion (+24.47% YoY), based on YCharts data.

Without putting a premium on that figure (following UBS analysts' skepticism) and assuming only 7x in EV/EBITDA for FY2027, I get an implied enterprise value of $24.248 billion. According to Seeking Alpha, the firm's balance sheet holds negative -$546 million in net debt, so based on a classic formula, equity value equals enterprise value minus net debt. But I treat the $680 million in pension obligations on the balance sheet as a debt-like item. Plus, we know that AA has ~$76 million in minority interest, so it should be subtracted as well. Here's what I got:

$24.248 - (-$0.546) - $0.680 - $0.076 = ~$24.038

The implied equity value arrives at ~$24.038 billion in the next 12 months or so.

The upside from today's market cap is about 49%, so my “Buy” rating remains unchanged.

Risks

The main risk to my bullish update and overall thesis lies in the cyclical nature of the aluminum and alumina markets. The thing is that if Alcoa indeed has a high sensitivity to its industry's changes, then even minor events can lead to a drop in its prospective EBITDA generation. That $215 million EBITDA impact from just a $100/mt increase in aluminum prices can work against Alcoa when prices drop and don't rise as bulls expect. I'd also add regulatory risks to the equation. And if AA's earnings drop in 2028 and 2029 the way the current consensus expects, the market might start to reprice the stock in advance, and AA might not reach that 7x EV/EBITDA multiple by the end of 2027.

Your Takeaway

Given that Alcoa doesn't look like a distressed aluminum producer anymore and it's rapidly entering the high-margin normalization phase of its business cycle, I believe the rally in the AA rally hasn't stopped yet, despite all the surrounding risks. I see a reason for a meaningful multiple expansion ahead, and if I'm right about it, we might see another 49% rise over the next 12 months. I keep my “Buy” unchanged for now.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: The Bullish And Bearish Case For 2026
data.description:
The year ahead presents both a bullish and bearish case for investors. Will 2026 be another year of above-average returns, or will it be a year of disappointment? The bulls argue that the key ingredients for a sustained rally are in place. A powerful technology cycle, aggressive corporate spending, and supportive policy measures all point to further gains. Conversely, the bears argue that key drivers are weakening, market leadership is dangerously narrow, and signs of economic strain are becoming increasingly visible beneath the surface.

Following a strong 2025, many investors are now facing a different market regime. Liquidity remains ample, but concerns around valuation, employment pressure, and consumer health are rising. The outcome depends on how long optimism can prevail over reality and whether the hoped-for gains from artificial intelligence and capital expenditures materialize in time to offset the economic drag from debt, interest rates, and inequality.

Sentiment indeed remains positive, although not universally so. Equity strategists are divided, and bond markets are pricing in both rate cuts and the risk of a recession. Furthermore, while fiscal stimulus could delay any downturn, it also adds to long-term imbalances. The challenge for investors is staying objective. While both the bull and bear cases have merit, the timing of outcomes will be critical, and the reality is that in 2026, both the bullish and bearish cases could be correct. Therefore, the right strategy will be the one that adapts.

Let’s break down both the bullish and bearish scenarios for 2026 and examine the arguments on each side. By assessing the macro and market drivers that shape each outlook, we can lay out clear, practical tactics to prepare your portfolio for either path. Whether the bullish or bearish case prevails in 2026, your edge will come from disciplined risk management, not from guessing the future.

The Bullish Case

The bullish case thesis is built on a few key pillars: a new wave of tech-driven investment, supportive fiscal policy, renewed liquidity, and the resilience of corporate and retail behavior. Combined, these forces have helped push markets higher, and bulls believe they will continue to do so well into 2026.

At the heart of the bull case is the emergence of a transformational technology cycle, anchored by artificial intelligence and infrastructure upgrades. Unlike past hype-driven tech cycles, this one is already producing real capital expenditure. The “Magnificent Seven” mega-cap firms have committed more than $600 billion toward data centers, semiconductors, and AI services. This spending has knock-on effects through software, energy, and industrial supply chains. If productivity gains follow, as many expect, earnings will expand and justify higher valuations.

Fiscal policy is also aligned with growth. Under a Trump-led government, tax cuts and direct payments are expected to stimulate both corporate and consumer sectors. The promise of $2,000 stimulus checks may not sound radical, but it boosts near-term consumption and supports small business revenues. Combined with income tax reductions, these measures provide a tailwind for GDP and investor sentiment. As shown, since the 2022 market correction and recession calls, fiscal stimulus has continued to provide steady support for economic growth.

The monetary backdrop is also shifting in favor of the bulls. Quantitative tightening ended in December 2025, and the Federal Reserve is now engaging in “Quantitative Easing Lite” as they continue to cut interest rates and buy $40 billion in short-dated Treasuries. The stated goal is “reserve management,” which is Fed-speak for ensuring there is ample liquidity in the financial system. As the Federal Reserve cuts rates, credit markets should ease, providing risk assets with a tailwind, and liquidity is expected to increase. This dynamic has historically driven higher equity multiples, particularly in technology and growth names.

Corporate behavior reinforces the trend. Share buyback authorizations are set to hit a new record of more than $1.2 trillion in 2026. While often quoted as a “capital return strategy,” which it isn’t, there is a clear correlation between buybacks and stock market performance. Particularly, since 2000, corporate buybacks have comprised nearly 100% of all net equity purchases.

Notably, the narrative that buybacks represent a confidence in future earnings is false; buybacks are being aggressively used to manipulate earnings to exceed Wall Street estimates. Financial engineering is set to expand further in 2026, providing additional support to operating earnings growth and the bullish case.

Lastly, there’s deregulation coming from the “Big Beautiful Bill,” which will ease capital rules on banks, allowing them to hold more collateral. While this provides a tailwind for the Treasury bond market, it also means more lending capacity will be available. Such lending capacity will find its way into leverage for hedge funds and Wall Street trading desks, as looser constraints will translate into an expansion of risk-taking.

The bullish case hinges on a tight feedback loop: innovation drives capital expenditures, which in turn boost earnings, policy injects liquidity, and investors respond with increased risk exposure. So long as each part holds, the trend can continue.

The Bearish Case

The bearish case begins with a critical point: many of the forces that drove 2025’s rally are either fading or already fully priced in. Notably, whether it is valuations, weakening economic indicators, or building speculative risks, the current market momentum may be blinding market participants to deeper structural cracks. However, let’s dig into a few of the issues.

Yes, one of the most obvious concerns is market concentration. Most of the gains in 2025 came from just 10 companies, on a market capitalization-weighted basis, which the massive shift into passive ETF investing fuels.

“Passive investing has grown from a niche strategy into the dominant force in equity markets. Index funds and ETFs now account for over half of U.S. equity ownership. These vehicles allocate capital based on market capitalization, not valuation, fundamentals, or business quality. As more money flows into these funds, the largest companies receive the lion’s share of new capital. That’s created a powerful feedback loop, where price drives flows, and flows drive price.“

This narrow leadership is inherently unsustainable. If something occurs that causes investor flows into ETFs to reverse, every dollar sold will pull 40% out of those same 10 companies. History shows that when markets rely on a few names for returns, volatility rises, and drawdowns can be severe.

Valuations are another warning sign. Price-to-earnings ratios on the S&P 500 remain near cycle highs. Growth expectations are lofty, and any disappointment could lead to repricing. AI enthusiasm has fueled a massive wave of investment, but much of it is circular, meaning that firms are spending on AI tools to sell AI products. That feedback loop may eventually hit limits, especially if demand softens or costs outpace returns.

Much of the current investment cycle is also debt-funded as companies borrow to invest, buy back shares, and maintain dividend levels. If rates stay elevated or credit conditions tighten, the cost of that debt could overwhelm earnings gains.

The larger economic concern is that the shift in capital allocation toward tech and automation may leave significant portions of the labor force behind. Yes, during the construction of “data centers,” we may see 5000 people employed, but only 500 are needed post-construction to operate the center. The long-term drag on employment growth will exert upward pressure on demand destruction, and we may already be seeing early signs of that.

Of course, this is the entire basis of the "K-shaped economy." In the current economy, high-income consumers and asset owners are doing well, but lower-income households are under pressure. As a result, consumption patterns are diverging, as lower-tier consumers reduce their spending. That leaves the top 20% of income earners to drive nearly 50% of current consumption. Already, defaults on auto loans and credit cards are rising, and real wages for many workers remain stagnant, even as housing and essential goods continue to be expensive.

Finally, there’s a growing risk in the credit system, especially in the private markets. Private credit has experienced significant growth in recent years, but the lack of transparency hinders its assessment of systemic risk. Regulators have begun to scrutinize this segment, and default rates in middle-market lending are rising. If defaults spread, the ripple effects could hit banks, hedge funds, and pension portfolios alike.

The bear case is not about imminent collapse. It’s about fragility. Beneath the headline gains lies a market vulnerable to earnings misses, credit tightening, and consumer weakness.

Here is the real catch: in 2026, we could see both the bullish and bearish cases. So, being prepared will be key.

Navigating Whatever Comes Our Way

Investors should approach 2026 as a year where both the bullish and bearish cases are proven correct. In the first half, bullish momentum is likely to continue driving gains. Sentiment remains strong, liquidity is ample, and corporate spending continues to ramp up. AI optimism, fiscal stimulus, and a potential pause in tightening may push indexes higher. However, by the second half, cracks could emerge. Valuation pressures are a concern as the risk of an earnings disappointment increases. Economic inequality puts pressure on future outlooks, particularly for corporate revenues. If that happens, sentiment could shift quickly.

To navigate a split year effectively, investors need to be tactical. The focus will be on capturing early-year upside without overexposing ourselves to potential second-half risks.

Early 2026: Ride Momentum, but Watch Position Size

  • Tilt toward sectors benefiting from capex and liquidity, such as technology, industrials, and energy.
  • Focus on high-quality growth stocks with strong earnings and cash flows, not hype.
  • Use trailing stop-losses to lock in gains if sentiment reverses.
  • Take advantage of short-term dislocations by adding during volatility, but reduce sizing as valuations expand.
  • Avoid overconcentration in AI names, even during rallies — dispersion risk rises with crowding.

Mid-to-Late 2026: Shift Toward Defense and Cash Flow Stability

  • Gradually rotate into value-oriented sectors such as healthcare, consumer staples, and utilities.
  • Increase exposure to dividend-paying companies with strong balance sheets.
  • Raise cash levels or short-duration Treasuries to maintain flexibility.
  • Allocate selectively to high-quality credit, while reducing exposure to private or high-yield debt.
  • Monitor consumer credit, employment trends, and bank earnings for early signs of stress.

Throughout the Year: Stay Disciplined and Objective

  • Stick to valuation rules regardless of narrative shifts.
  • Maintain a diversified portfolio that can absorb both volatility and rotation.
  • Use data, not headlines, to guide allocations.
  • Rebalance regularly, primarily if the first half produces substantial gains that overweight specific sectors.

Tactical flexibility, risk awareness, and discipline will matter more in 2026 than simple bullish or bearish positioning. It is a year where potentially both bulls and bears could be wrong. Historically, markets may not follow a straight line, but your management process should.

2026 will test investors in terms of increased volatility as both the bullish and bearish cases have substance. Yes, a new technology cycle creates real economic momentum, but it also brings risks associated with overstretched valuations, debt-driven growth, and growing social inequality. Markets are pricing in perfection, and historically speaking, such rarely ends as expected.

Whether the year brings another rally or a sharp correction, your results will depend on how well you manage risk. Don’t anchor to either narrative; watch the data, follow your signals, and adjust as needed.

Remember, your investment goal isn’t to chase market returns, but rather to survive and prosper over the range of market cycles.

Trade accordingly.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Vetting The +11% Yield: Why Blue Owl Capital Is Better Today Than In 2021
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

We live in an incredible world. That little machine that you carry in your pocket provides you with a massive amount of computing power and near-instant access to an enormous amount of information.

Recently, I was doing a minor renovation and putting in a new power outlet. Not a terribly complicated task, but one that I definitely wanted to ensure was done correctly. After all, I prefer my house not to burn down. So, I went to the old YouTube, pulled up some instructional videos, and got a quick tutorial that confirmed what I was already doing. It's something that I do quite frequently, whether I'm working on my car or repairing something around the house.

However, a wise sage once said, With great utility comes great problems.

There's a lot of bad information on the internet. Recently, the City of St. Louis Department of Health was chasing monkeys that officials aren't certain were ever real, as people flooded the internet with fake AI-generated images.

You can't believe everything you see and read on the Internet. To some extent, that has always been true. However, it used to be that it took an extremely skilled person a lot of time to generate images that weren't obviously photoshopped. Something AI can now do in minutes.

As investors, it is an important reminder to us to make sure that we are getting our information from high-quality sources, that we are vetting the quality of the sources we rely on, and that we take the time to truly understand the investments we are buying.

Today, we're going to talk about Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), yielding 11.7%. It is a BDC that is managed by Blue Owl (OWL). Many people in the comments were conflating the two companies. OBDC is a BDC that is externally managed by OWL and represents just one of numerous businesses that OWL manages. OBDC is the second-largest publicly traded BDC, and it focuses on lending to the "upper middle market". These are mostly private businesses, and OBDC's portfolio companies have an average revenue of over $1 billion/year and an average EBITDA of $229 million. BDCs focus on "small" businesses, but these aren't exactly mom & pop stores that OBDC is investing in. They are businesses that have built up a regional and sometimes even national presence, and 91% of them are backed by a PE (Private Equity) sponsor. PE provides the equity investment, while OBDC provides the debt.

OBDC

The last time we talked about OBDC, the stock was in freefall post earnings. We wrote that we were eager to buy OBDC at a +15% discount to NAV.

Fast forward to today, and with the benefit of 20/20 hindsight, we can see that the market's panic was short-lived. Based on comments in our public article, the panic was over the risk of "private capital" and the allegedly "risky" joint venture that the manager OWL made with some newfangled trendy company; maybe you've heard of it: META (META).

Yet, for all the wailing and gnashing of teeth, the price did dip about 10%, and then it recovered within a month.

Welcome to the stock market. This is why we don't hyperventilate over stock prices. Common equities are volatile. It is an inherent reality of a highly liquid market where the only barriers to entry are a smartphone and $1.

What we frequently see with high-yield investments is a lot of obsession with the share price and ignoring the fact that the total return comes from the dividend. Here is the past five years of OBDC, which is pretty close to when we added it to the portfolio.

The price went down through 2022. Then it went up and peaked in mid-2024, and yes, it declined quite a bit in 2025, along with the brief dip in November - to end about 5% down over five years. Meanwhile, the total return over the same period is over 60%.

When we bought OBDC in January 2021, we were buying a company that was paying $1.24/share in dividends, good for a 10% yield. We were paid more than that as OBDC raised its dividend and paid out numerous supplements along the way.

Today, OBDC is trading at roughly the same price we paid five years ago. Let's look at what has changed for the company's fundamentals.

Earnings In 2025 Vs. 2020

OBDC reported NII/share (Net Investment Income) of $0.33, was paying $0.31/share in regular distributions, and had a NAV of $14.67 as of Q3 2020: Source

As of Q3 2025, OBDC has $0.37 of NII/share, pays a dividend of $0.37/quarter, and has a NAV of $14.89. Source

In 2020, 1% of OBDC's portfolio was on non-accrual (at fair value), with 88% of the portfolio in the two best risk ratings. In 2025, non-accruals were 1.3% of fair value, with 91.7% of the investment at the two best risk ratings.

In 2020, OBDC's portfolio consisted of 110 companies with an average asset yield of 7.9% and a 47% LTV (loan-to-value). In 2025, OBDC's portfolio consisted of 238 companies with an average yield of 9.8% and a 42% LTV.

In short, by whatever objective measure you decide to come up with, looking at the earnings numbers, OBDC in Q3 2025 is clearly a superior company to Q3 2020.

What changed is sentiment. In late 2020/early 2021, investors were more eager for yield and willing to pay higher prices for dividends - something that accelerated and peaked in early 2022. Over the past few years, interest rates have been higher, and yield is more readily available. Plentiful things aren't appreciated until they stop being so readily available.

Today, OBDC is a better company by any measure. It is earning more money, has a higher book value, is paying a higher dividend, and is trading at the same price. Our evaluation hasn't changed. Unlike the market, we value having cash flow at all times. Whether it is 2021, 2025, 2026, or 2036, we want to have dividends pouring into our portfolio. OBDC does that.

2026 Outlook

Looking forward, we do need to recognize that OBDC has paid out more in recent years due to the rapid rise in interest rates. Management discussed it very openly at the Q3 earnings call, and we appreciate the transparency.

"But as we look to 2026, given how much rates have come down or expected to come down, it's logical for investors to think that we'll evaluate reducing the dividend appropriate with the earnings power in a lower rate environment. So we'll look at it. We have a strong performing portfolio. And our dividend levels for investors who are newer to the stock were lower in a lower-rate environment. If you went back to when rates were at 3%, our dividend was about $0.33.

So it's -- there are good data points that investors can look to, to try to calibrate where dividends will go. We enjoy the benefit of higher rates, but this -- we think this company is designed to generate a premium return in all rate environments. It's not designed to generate a high level of return when rates are low because of the quality we're investing in, in the market we invest in, the rate -- the investment opportunities fluctuate. The absolute return fluctuates based on where rates are.

So I think you can look at where our rates were at a 3% environment at $0.33 and get a sense of the order of magnitude of what we might consider. We're not doing that now. We're not going to do that for the fourth quarter. And we'll have discussions with our Board as 2026 gets underway, based on rate expectations at the time to consider what we do with the dividend."

So, as earnings are coming up, there is a probability that management either discusses the dividend or that a decision is made to reduce it at Q4 earnings. OBDC pays out substantially all of its earnings, and earnings have been inflated. It actually has a policy of paying out a supplement if earnings exceed the dividend. So, we can be confident as shareholders that our reward will be aligned with whatever the portfolio is producing.

We think OBDC is a buy today. We bought it for $12.31 when it was paying $0.31/quarter. We're certainly happy to pay these prices if the assumption is that the dividend will be $0.33/quarter going forward. OBDC is going to pay out a high yield, and that yield is going to be based on earnings, which are going to vary. If we see OBDC announce a change next month and the price reacts, we'll be buying.

Know what you're buying. BDCs have seen a windfall in earnings because they lend at floating rates and borrow at fixed rates. Due to the Fed's aggressive rate hikes, BDCs saw a spike in earnings. It was never going to be sustainable. Either rates would be cut, or over time, the BDCs would have to refinance their fixed-rate debt at higher rates.

We appreciate that OBDC management has been clear on this issue and has been willing to quantify what they think the impact might be. As investors, we should listen to management and decide if OBDC is worth the price it is trading at. For us, the answer is definitive. We would be perfectly happy buying OBDC at +$15. We are thrilled to be able to buy it cheaper.

data.title: Columbia Banking: After Q4 Results, More Reasons To Buy As Western Growth Continues
data.description:
Columbia Banking System (COLB), a regional bank in the Pacific Northwest and the parent of Columbia Bank, recently published its most recent earnings results, and the stock is up around +6.5% since I called it a buy in my August 2025 article, a bullish view driven heavily by strong profit margins and growth through recent acquisitions.

Thesis Summary

My updated thesis reaffirms my prior buy rating on this stock from last summer, arguing that there is considerable upside potential for this bank after a major acquisition in 2025, recent growth in new deposits and loan originations, some improved macro forecasts for the regional bank sector, and a strong investment-grade rating from Fitch along with a well-diversified commercial real estate loan portfolio the bank is exposed to.

The following updated and revised rating worksheet shows how I got to this rating, and in the following sections I'll go over some actionable insights about each of these categories reviewed.

Actionable Insights

Macro & Sector Outlook

The US regional banking sector is back in focus again for me in 2026, and for good reason. A key actionable insight to consider in this niche is what Morningstar wrote about just last month, citing Catherine Mealor of Keefe, Bruyette & Woods/KBW, who remarked that although "growth has been delayed in 2025, in part because of tariff noise in April," it appears that 2026 could see a turnaround, as "merger activity for regional banks is taking off because of the health of the industry and regulatory easing. A higher level of M&A activity supports stock prices."

I think this could drive further tailwinds for a bank like Columbia, which has already been on a merger spree with its acquisition of Pacific Premier Bancorp, which was completed last autumn, and the upside could come from brand expansion and new deposit accounts and loans, which matters because loan portfolio growth can drive future revenue. Hence, I think the stock is a buy in this category.

Competitive Positioning

Within the regional banking sector, I picked the following 3 peers to compare against as they are in the $6B - $10B market cap range and cover different regions of the US: Cullen/Frost (CFR) in Texas, Valley National Bancorp (VLY) in the northeast, and UMB (UMBF) in Kansas City.

Because they are in a similar market cap range and serve different regions of the US, for regional bank investors, I think a metric that could differentiate one from another is how well they generate return on equity.

From the chart I made below, within this peer group, Columbia has been trending towards the lower end of the group with a return on equity of 8.3%, with the trend over the last year actually pointing downward:

Cullen-Frost, a bank I am familiar with during my time living and working in Austin, Texas, actually showed a steady return on equity in the +15% range for that same period.

When it comes to revenue growth for a 1-year period, Columbia was at nearly +18% but fell behind its peer UMB, who topped +45%:

So, I am calling this stock a hold in this category, as its positioning within this specific peer group could use improvement.

Company Growth Factors

Moving on, we can think about company-specific growth factors. We already mentioned the acquisition of Pacific Premier, for instance, which is an example of recent business growth that can drive future upside.

Since regional banks depend heavily on customer deposits for funding, but deposit customers can also drive new clients that may later use other products (loans, credit cards, etc.), notable from the FY25 Q4 quarterly results (pg. 8) is that "Columbia generated $1.3B in new customer deposits through our three successful small business and retail campaigns."

That is a positive upside driver, I think, and further from the Q4 investor deck (pg. 12), I would highlight that in FY25 the bank saw higher loan originations than in the prior year:

So, based on business acquisitions, deposit growth, and loan origination growth (which could drive future interest income), I am calling the stock a strong buy in this category.

Profitability Metrics

From the following peer comparison I made, Columbia is somewhere in the middle of this peer group with a profit margin of +15.9%, and we also see in this 5-year period that these peers have seen an improving trend lately after their margins took a dip in recent years:

I think if this trend continues, Columbia can recover back to its margin levels pre-2024, driving greater earnings efficiency.

What is key to mention about Columbia is that it recently acquired another bank, so we have to think about short-term integration costs involved, which I think should see upside in the longer run. In fact, in his Q4 comments, CEO Clint Stein said, "We remain on track for a seamless systems conversion later this quarter, which will enable us to fully realize deal-related cost savings and achieve a clean expense run rate by the third quarter."

Of course, there is always execution risk in every post-merger environment. Personally, I went through the Charles Schwab (SCHW)-TD Ameritrade merger ecosystem several years ago firsthand, seeing some of the integration challenges that could transpire as with any combination involving systems, people, and processes, but also brand or platform loyalty, so this merger can also have at least some short-term integration risk to consider.

With that said, I gave the stock a hold in this category, given the combined picture above.

Balance Sheet Risk

With a regional bank like this, when thinking about balance sheet risk exposure and what it could mean for the future, what first comes to mind as someone who often writes about REITs is what kind of commercial real estate loans this bank is exposed to.

In my research on REITs, I learned that "defensive" real estate usually includes multi-family residential, while properties like hotels can be considered more cyclical and recession-prone. With that said, the good news is that, according to the bank's Q4 investor deck (pg. 14), its largest CRE exposure happens to be multifamily, with the smallest exposure being in hotels:

Further, while all banks I've written about on this website seem to have some level of leverage risk, let's compare Columbia with its peers I picked in terms of debt vs equity:

In this comparison, Columbia has the lowest D/E at just 0.39, indicating a much lower leverage in relation to equity, and I think that is an upside factor that positions it more favorably for future debt issuance.

In addition, to get some risk perspective from a third-party credit rating agency, I turned to a note last spring from Fitch, which gave this bank an investment-grade "BBB+" rating, saying that "the affirmation and stable outlook reflect Fitch's view that strategic benefits of the merger, including greater scale efficiencies and increased geographic diversity."

Considering the highly diversified and lower-risk profile of the CRE loan portfolio, the low D/E ratio in this peer group, and the strong rating from Fitch, I gave Columbia a strong buy in this category.

Dividend Outlook

When it comes to the dividend outlook, the company had some positive guidance in the Q4 investor deck (pg. 18), where it said, "We expect to organically generate capital above what is required to support prudent growth and our regular dividend."

That guidance should add confidence to investors and analysts questioning whether the dividend can be covered adequately.

As for the growth story, Seeking Alpha data supports my view that this bank has been a steady dividend grower over 10 years, with a 10-year dividend CAGR of +7.8%, slightly above the sector average, actually. Although that is historical data, I believe it makes the case that this bank has been resilient enough over a longer period of many market shocks (pandemics, inflation, interest rate shocks, tariffs, etc.) to sustain its longer-term dividend growth.

For dividend investors focused more on yield, however, with a fwd div yield around +5%, it is higher than the 3 peers I am comparing today in the regional bank sector.

So, with positive company guidance, a proven dividend growth, and a yield above several regional banks, I am calling it a buy in this category.

Valuation Metrics

Let's go over some valuation metrics in relation to future upside potential, using my worksheet below, which uses data from Seeking Alpha valuation and consensus earnings estimates.

Based on this worksheet I made, because this is not a REIT but a bank, the valuation metrics that matter to me are fwd P/E and trailing P/B multiples, both of which are considerably undervalued vs this peer group average, and I think driving that lower premium by the market could be somewhat weaker profit margins than some peers but also exposure to loans on office properties too, which I showed in my CRE infographic earlier. That added risk perception could be baked into this valuation.

As far as my price target, it implies a 2% further upside to the share price, which supports a "hold" based on my price target guide shown, a new feature I am providing to readers as an additional actionable insight.

So, although this section is not the deciding factor in the overall score, I called the stock a hold in this category due to justified undervaluation combined with very limited upside forecasted.

Technical Charting

In my updated analysis approach today, I left technical charting last after considering multiple fundamental analysis topics.

Consider the technical chart I created below comparing movement of the price vs the 50-day and 200-day SMA over the last 1-year period:

We can see a sell signal (death cross) that formed around last April and a buy signal (golden cross) that formed in the autumn just before October, and since then the two moving averages have been parallel.

However, they also seem to be sloping upward, and the 50-day SMA is providing ample support to the share price, which trends above it.

Therefore, while at first glance one could argue for a hold, when considering the slope and where the price is, I went with a buy in this category. A bearish call obviously would not be justified, as there is no clear sell signal that is appearing. This could occur if the 50-day crosses below the 200-day like last April, or I can get ahead of that if it starts sloping downward and with the appearance of an upcoming death cross forming.

Risks and Challenge to Thesis

A key challenge to my bullish view of this stock, not included in this rating score, is the potential downside risk of interest rate decisions by the Fed since that is outside the control of the bank yet certainly can impact this sector.

The future downside could come from both the Fed raising and lowering rates, as regional banks can face risks like greater interest margin compression, lower yields on interest-earning assets (loans, fixed-income securities), and also the risk of higher loan defaults if too many borrowers struggle to pay the high rates. Interest rate movements also can impact the value of fixed-income securities a bank may hold on its books.

A mitigating factor to this interest rate risk is that the bank could grow its non-interest, fees-driven business more robustly. A positive trend appearing in the income statement data is that this bank has seen growth in non-interest income in the quarterly comparison between June 2025 and Dec. 2025.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I've shown several reasons that support a bullish view of this stock once again, driven by top-line growth factors (business growth, loan and deposit growth, and brand expansion in the West) as well as an attractive balance sheet risk profile, a proven dividend story, a favorable view by Fitch, and technical chart patterns supporting a bullish view.

I recommend adding this stock to your watchlist of regional banks to research further, as well as a dividend idea for this month.

Rating Methodology

Insights by Albert Anthony is the author's research approach that aims to tell a holistic story about a stock, starting with the larger macro/sector overview and narrowing down to a detailed technical price trend, focused on concisely answering questions like what could move the stock in the future, what is its market advantage, and does its valuation make sense in relation to future upside potential.

data.title: IBM Earnings Preview: Operating Margin Is Slowly Expanding
data.description:
IBM Corp. (IBM) is scheduled to report their Q4 ’25 financial results after the closing bell on Wednesday, January 28th, 2026.

Microsoft (MSFT), Tesla (TSLA) and Meta (META) are also scheduled to report Wednesday night, January 28th, after the market closes, so there will be enough tech reports to satisfy everyone.

The reason for the interest in IBM from this blog is that since the stock peaked in mid-April 2013 near $215 per share, IBM has returned 7.11%, versus the S&P 500’s 14.45% return the last 12 years. A lot of IBM's return has come since July 1 ’24, when the stock was still trading under $170. The former mainframe giant didn’t take out that $215 high until the fourth quarter of 2024, and since breaking out, returned +37.8% in calendar ’25 versus the S&P 500’s +17.8% return.

After 12 years, IBM is finally generating “alpha” for investors, and at least in terms of its historical tech cycle (discussed below), there should be more upside to go for the stock.

Sell-side consensus is looking for $4.32 in EPS and $19.2 billion in revenue, as well as $4.9 bl in operating income, for expected year-over-year (y-o-y) growth of 10%, 9% and 17% respectively.

For 2026, IBM consensus is expecting 5% revenue growth and 8% EPS growth for the full year 2026.

Without burying the lede, and to make for a hopefully short and sweet earnings preview, IBM’s operating margin has been slowly expanding this decade, and also capex has been shrinking, which has boosted free cash flow, so take a look at the following numbers.

IBM operating margin

  • 4-qtr avg: 19.9%
  • 12-qtr avg: 17.6%
  • 20-qtr avg: 15.8%
  • 40-qtr avg: 15.7%

The big change the last 5 years has been the full integration of RedHat, not to mention the emergence of the AI opportunity. In the June ’25 quarter, the “AI book” at IBM was $7.5 bl, while at the end of the September ’25 quarter, the AI book was pegged at $9.5 bl (cumulatively).

Another big change is the IBM business model transition from a mainframe and hardware business to a significant software business, which is now mid-40% of IBM’s total revenue. (Morningstar refers to IBM’s software segment as “its largest business segment”, but also notes that it doesn’t expect the software segment to become greater than 50% of revenue until 2029.)

Capex has been shrinking

Looking at the cash flow statement, here’s a look at IBM’s annual capex for the decade so far -

  • 2025: $1.8 bl (through 9 months)
  • 2024: $881 million
  • 2023: $1.492 billion
  • 2022: $1.7 billion
  • 2021: $2.2 billion
  • 2020: $2.6 billion

The lowered capex has led to these free cash flow (FCF) numbers for the same period:

  • 2025: $14.2 billion (est.) (LSEG has a $6.8 bl free cash flow estimate for Q4 ’25)
  • 2024: $13.0 billion
  • 2023: $12.4 billion
  • 2022: $6.9 billion
  • 2021: $7.5 billion
  • 2020: $10.4 billion
  • For the entire decade from 2010 to 2019, IBM averaged $3.3 billion per quarter in free cash flow.
  • Both the capex and the FCF data are taken from an internal spreadsheet. Any errors or conflicts with sell-side data are my own.
  • Large acquisitions - like RedHat - are typically considered part of capex, but the small acquisitions are not added back to capex.

Valuation

At $292 per share, IBM is trading at 26x and 24 expected EPS in ’25 and ’26 of $11.35 and $12.32 per share, for expected EPS growth of 10% and 8% respectively.

The notable metric about 2025 is that revenue growth hit 7%, IBM’s best year of revenue growth since 2011 (!) - that was surprising to find on the spreadsheet.

Another notable stat readers might find interesting is that of the 16 years (2010-2025 inclusive), IBM has had negative year-over-year revenue growth in 10 of those years. The next highest year of revenue growth was +2% in 2010.

IBM hasn’t bought back any stock since $2 bl was repo’ed in Q4 ’18 and then $920 ml was repo’ed in Q1 ’19.

The cash flow and free cash flow valuation as of 9/30/25 for IBM were 22x and 26x respectively, and Big Blue sported a 4% free cash flow yield as of the October ’25 earnings report.

IBM is one of two big-cap tech companies (the other being Oracle (ORCL)) that benefit from a weaker dollar. That may have been one reason IBM is looking to finish off ’25 with 7% revenue growth, and with the weak dollar continuing into ’26, the expected estimated 5% revenue growth for ’26 could be sweetened a little. Granted, I’d rather see 7% pure organic growth, but the weak dollar does help at the margin.

Summary / conclusion

IBM stock price usually runs in 8-, 10-, 12-year cycles of strong outperformance to capital destruction and returns that rival memory stocks:

  • 1987 - Stock peaks near $180-190 (from memory) prior to the ’87 crash. For the rest of the decade, server and workstation buildout through Corporate America decimates the mainframe business, and all of the early 1990s were disastrous for the stock as PCs, servers, and workstations multiplied like rabbits.
  • 1995 - Lou Gerstner named CEO, after running Nabisco; acquires Lotus 1-2-3, and makes some other acquisitions for IBM, finally generating some earnings and revenue growth for the mainframe giant. The stock performs better in the late 1990s.
  • 2002 - Sam Palmisano replaces Lou Gerstner in 2002, sets IBM on a course involving “big data” and technology services. During Sam’s tenure, IBM stock price performance is double that of the S&P 500.
  • Sam Palmisano resigns 12/31/2011, handing Ginny Rometty a total bag of sand, just as the “cloud/mobile/social” transformation was beginning in tech.
  • Ginny Rometty saw IBM stock price peak in April 2013 and then languish for most of her tenure as the company struggled to offset the secular decline in “tech services”.
  • Arvind Krishna appointed IBM CEO on April 6, 2020, replacing Ginny Rometty. IBM stock finally breaks out above the April 2013 all-time high of $215 in late 2024.

Apologies for the history lesson, but there’s two ways you can evaluate the highs and lows of IBM: you can look at CEOs’ tenures, or you can look at the technology cycles that proliferate our wonderful business and determine if IBM can position themselves to benefit in some way, shape or form.

Under Arvind Krishna, IBM is positioning to gain some revenue from AI, as the AI book was $9.5 billion as of the September ’25 quarter, supposedly 22% of the consulting book.

This blog never owned the stock probably from early 2014 until the ramp in 2024, but got my attention as the stock headed towards its old high. I tried a few times to get in and out of it or pick a bottom, but the inevitable IBM cycle constantly re-asserted itself - and not in a good way - until late 2024.

If you think it’s easy to trade the great giants like IBM, Katy Huberty, a big-time technology strategist and stock picker at Morgan Stanley, made a notable buy call on IBM when the stock was trading around $115-120 in the first quarter of 2016 (crude oil had fallen from $94 in late 2014 to bottom in Q1 ’16 near $28 per barrel, and it rattled everything and everyone on the Street), putting a price target on Big Blue of $165-170 (again, from memory) - and the stock took years to take out that level.

Personally, I think we are early in the “new” IBM cycle. Arvind is supposedly very capable and is re-positioning the company to benefit from AI, possibly Quantum, and what else may follow in the next few years.

Where does IBM reach “full value”? Hard to say, over $300, or $350 or $400 - in past cycles, the stock has outperformed.

In past cycles, IBM has had excess cash flow to repurchase stock and boost EPS, but for some reason - at least so far - the CEO and CFO are unwilling to repurchase any stock. IBM’s balance sheet cash was 5% of the market cap of the stock after the October ’25 financial results were released. Management is likely being prudent and might look to pay down the $47 bl in term debt outstanding, $20 bl of which is from the RedHat acquisition.

Readers should look for continued revenue growth y-o-y, continued margin expansion as software closes in on 50% of IBM revenue, and possibly better free cash flow in coming quarters and years.

This blog has a 2.5% position in IBM spread across client portfolios. Like the Boeing earnings preview, these old American giants can make for great turnaround stories, with significant alpha generation, without getting tied up in the AI momo (momentum) stocks.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. LSEG I/B/E/S is the source for all EPS, revenue, operating income, and free cash flow estimates cited above.
